comments:
Summary
This paper develops a systematic framework for what it means to be a “good” CNF (SAT) representation of a Boolean function. Building on earlier work, the author defines and studies three hierarchies of clause-sets — UCk (unit-refutation complete at level k), PCk (propagation-complete at level k) and WCk (width-refutation complete at level k) — together with measures of hardness that characterise them. The paper investigates representation under two conditions (absolute vs relative) and with/without auxiliary variables. Main technical contributions include: (i) a proof that the UCk and WCk hierarchies are strict with respect to equivalent representations without new variables (exponential separations between adjacent levels); (ii) the introduction and analysis of minimal premise sets and a “doping” construction that links these to prime implicates; (iii) a lower-bounding technique using trigger hypergraphs and extremal trees; (iv) an analysis of Tseitin-style translations (canonical and reduced canonical) with guarantees about membership in UC; (v) experimental evidence comparing three representation strategies on crafted families. The paper concludes with many open problems and conjectures, several of which are well-motivated.

Strengths
- Ambitious, comprehensive and technically deep. The author develops a rich theory tying together proof complexity (tree resolution, width), knowledge compilation, and SAT translation practice. The separation results (Theorem 6.14 and supporting lemmas) are nontrivial and appear to settle an important question about expressiveness of the UC hierarchy without new variables.
- The “doping” idea and the connection between minimal premise subsets and prime implicates is elegant and seems broadly useful for constructing hard examples with controlled structure.
- The trigger-hypergraph lower-bound method is a clean combinatorial tool and the extremal-tree construction is a natural way to obtain large families of prime implicates while controlling hardness.
- Analysis of Tseitin (canonical and reduced) translations is useful: it clarifies when introducing auxiliary variables helps (and when canonical translation can create hard subproblems), and shows that the reduced canonical translation yields UC representations for arbitrary DNFs.
- The paper contains explicit conjectures and an extensive list of open problems, providing a clear research agenda.

Weaknesses and points for improvement
- Length and density: the paper is long and highly technical. While this is intrinsic to the subject, some readers would benefit from a shorter high-level overview (perhaps a 2–3 page roadmap) stating key definitions, theorems and intuitions before diving into detailed proofs.
- Exposition could be improved in places: many important concepts and distinctions (absolute vs relative condition, the role of new variables, connections to standard notions like arc-consistency) appear repeatedly; a central table or diagram summarising the relationships between PCk, UCk, WCk, hd/whd/phd, and relative/absolute would aid comprehension.
- Experimental evaluation is rather limited: the experiments use carefully constructed instances (the natural testbed), and show solver behaviour differences, but they are small in scope. A more varied experimental study (more solvers, more instance families and scaled sizes, and more detailed profiling of the impact of preprocessing and failed-literal elimination) would strengthen the practical claims.
- Some conjectures remain open and important (e.g., whether hierarchies collapse when new variables are allowed under relative condition; whether XOR systems admit bounded-hardness representations). These are presented clearly, but empirical or partial theoretical evidence for/against some conjectures (beyond current examples) would be valuable.

Soundness and correctness
I found the paper carefully written and mathematically rigorous. The arguments appear sound on the level of this review; however many proofs are intricate and I have not formally verified every step. I therefore rate correctness conservatively as high but not maximal. Several lemmas and constructions are interdependent; the exposition cites earlier papers by the author for background results, which is appropriate. The critical new arguments (trigger hypergraphs, extremal trees, Theorem 6.14) are well motivated and plausible.

Practical relevance
The paper frames a concrete path towards better SAT translations: pre-compiling constraint encodings into targeted UCk-level clause-sets (without new variables when feasible) or using controlled Tseitin encodings where auxiliary variables are necessary, guided by the theoretical trade-offs between size and inference power. The work clarifies when standard translations (e.g., Tseitin) are safe and when they may produce hard subproblems. However, more practical guidance (algorithms, heuristics) for automatically finding small UCk-representations for real constraints would be a useful addition in future work.

Suggestions
1. Add a concise roadmap/summary at the start (1–2 pages) listing new definitions, main theorems, and their significance. 2. Include a diagram/table mapping classes (PCk, UCk, WCk), hardness measures, and relative vs absolute conditions. 3. Expand experiments: test more solvers, larger instances and more representation heuristics; provide runtime profiles and, if possible, measures of propagation effort (e.g., number of unit-propagation steps, failed-literal invocations). 4. Provide pseudocode or at least an outline for practical compilation heuristics (how to search for low-hardness equivalent representations without new variables). 5. Minor editorial cleanup in some dense paragraphs would improve readability.

Conclusion and recommendation
This is a strong, thorough and original contribution to the theory of SAT representations and knowledge compilation. It combines proof complexity, combinatorics and practical considerations in a convincing way. While dense, the paper will be of high value to researchers working on SAT translation, proof complexity, and knowledge compilation, and it opens many promising directions. I recommend acceptance and wide dissemination. (Recommendation number expresses relative enthusiasm on a 1–10 scale.)
